You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Character (black L-shape with white dots) starts at (15,18)-(23,26), moves 3 cells per action - Dark gray L-shape at (15,36)-(23,44), moves opposite horizontally, same direction vertically - Yellow L-shape target at (45,51)-(53,59) - likely the goal - Sky blue divider at cols 30-32 - character can pass through - Yellow bar at col 63 shrinks by 1 per action (HUD/timer) - Movement: RIGHT moves char right 3, dark left 3; DOWN moves both down 3
- Plan: Navigate character to overlap the yellow L-shape. Do 11 RIGHTs first (to avoid collision with dark object), then 10 DOWNs.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, after the ACTION7 rotation, the character moved up (as expected) and the yellow shape seems to have reappeared in its full form. The character is now above the yellow L-shape again.
Let me check the exact positions to understand what happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check positions after rotation
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 40:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Character: bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
        for c in n['children']:
            cn = [x for x in seg if x['id'] == c][0]
            cb = cn['boundary']
            print(f"  Child {c}: color={cn['color']}, pixels={cn['pixels']}, pos=({cb[0][0]},{cb[0][1]})")
    elif n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] > 5 and n['pixels'] < 50:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Yellow shape: bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Yellow shape: bbox=(33,63)-(63,63), pixels=31
Character: bbox=(42,51)-(50,59), children=[7, 8, 9, 11, 12]
  Child 7: color=W, pixels=1, pos=(43,52)
  Child 8: color=W, pixels=1, pos=(43,55)
  Child 9: color=W, pixels=1, pos=(43,58)
  Child 11: color=Y, pixels=1, pos=(46,58)
  Child 12: color=W, pixels=1, pos=(49,58)
Yellow shape: bbox=(45,51)-(53,56), pixels=36

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting. After the rotation:
- Character moved up from (45,51)-(53,59) to (42,51)-(50,59)
- The yellow shape is back at (45,51)-(53,56) with 36 pixels (was 18 before)
- The character lost 2 yellow children and gained 1 white child (now has 4 white, 1 yellow)
So when the character rotated, it moved up and the yellow shape "regenerated" or the overlap was undone. This suggests:
1. The character absorbs yellow pixels when overlapping in the correct orientation
2. When the character moves away or rotates, the yellow shape regenerates
3. The goal is to have the character fully overlap the yellow shape in the correct orientation
The character needs to:
1. Be at position (45,51)-(53,59)
2. Have the same orientation as the yellow L-shape
Currently after 4 rotations total (3 before + 1 after moving down), the character is at (42,51)-(50,59). I need to move it down 1 more time to get to (45,51)-(53,59).
But wait - the character's orientation after 4 rotations: if each rotation is 90 degrees, 4 rotations = 360 degrees = back to original orientation. But the character's children show it has a different orientation now (white dots at top row, one yellow dot, and white dots going down the right side).
Let me think about this differently. The yellow L-shape has its corner at top-left. The character, to match, needs its corner at top-left too.
Looking at the child positions:
- White dots at (43,52), (43,55), (43,58) - horizontal row at top
- Yellow dot at (46,58) - middle of right column
- White dot at (49,58) - bottom of right column
This is the same orientation as before: horizontal bar on top, vertical bar on right. The corner is at top-right.
The yellow L-shape has its corner at top-left. So the character needs to be mirrored or rotated differently.
Wait, maybe the goal isn't to match orientation but to collect all yellow pixels. Let me try moving down once more to overlap again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move down once to overlap yellow shape again
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
